articles.title: Trump’s election-power campaign enters the courts, the states, and the 2026 campaign trail
articles.summary: A new push by the Justice Department, the White House and allied Republicans to reshape election rules is colliding with lawsuits, state resistance and looming midterm deadlines.

articles.title: The Public Culture Shock: Why America’s arts and media lifelines are being forced to reinvent themselves at once
articles.summary: A wave of federal defunding, court fights, budget stress, and audience fragmentation is reshaping the institutions that make culture public, local, and shared.

articles.title: America’s AI power crunch is no longer hypothetical
articles.summary: From Virginia substations to chip fabs in Arizona and Taiwan, the bottleneck in AI has shifted from model quality to electricity, transformers, and permitting.

articles.title: Tariffs are back in the price data. What that means for household budgets
articles.summary: March inflation, producer prices and import costs all moved higher in April 2026, adding evidence that tariff pressure is filtering through the pricing chain and may spill from wholesale markets into household budgets.

articles.title: Why April 2026 is a make-or-break month for FAFSA, attendance, and the pipeline to college
articles.summary: Federal aid, chronic absenteeism, and state accountability systems are colliding again — and schools are being asked to do more with less.

articles.title: Measles surge exposes fragile vaccine defenses as U.S. nears a status review
articles.summary: A sharp rise in U.S. measles cases, slipping childhood vaccination coverage, and outbreaks from Bangladesh to the Americas are testing how much protection remains when routine immunization gaps widen and public-health systems thin.

articles.title: The Case Against Outsourcing Moral Judgment to Machines
articles.summary: As governments, employers, courts, and hospitals rush to fold AI into public life, the real philosophical question is no longer whether machines can think. It is whether democratic societies are willing to let systems that cannot bear responsibility make decisions humans must still be able to justify.

articles.title: The science of March heat and the limits of attribution
articles.summary: A record-shattering warm spell across the continental United States is colliding with a bigger scientific debate: how far can researchers go in tying a single extreme month to climate change, and what still resists precise explanation?

articles.title: Sudan’s war pushes Chad to the brink as border violence, hunger and aid cuts collide
articles.summary: A widening Sudan spillover is turning eastern Chad into a stress test for border security, refugee protection and humanitarian finance across the Sahel.

articles.title: America’s Data-Center Boom Needs Rules Before It Needs More Praise
articles.summary: The U.S. should stop treating AI infrastructure as an unalloyed good and start regulating it like the power-hungry public utility it has become.

articles.title: AI’s power problem: the new bottleneck is no longer chips alone
articles.summary: A surge in data-center demand, new federal and state scrutiny, and rising local resistance are forcing governments and utilities to confront the physical costs of the AI boom.

articles.title: Europe’s Russian gas cutoff turns sanctions into infrastructure policy
articles.summary: A new EU ban on Russian gas is remaking Europe’s energy map, tightening sanctions logic while exposing how geography, storage, ports, pipelines, and political unity still shape deterrence.

articles.title: CinemaCon 2026 puts theatrical movies back at the center of Hollywood’s fight for the future
articles.summary: At this week’s CinemaCon in Las Vegas, studios pitched a bigger theatrical future even as consolidation, shrinking slates, and streaming habits keep the business under pressure.

articles.title: AI Music Flood Forces Pop Culture Platforms to Choose Between Growth and Trust
articles.summary: Deezer’s claim that a large share of new uploads are AI-generated shows synthetic music has become a platform-economics problem, a copyright fight, and a test of whether streaming can still reward human taste.

articles.title: Amateur radio’s club revival is a data-rich test of whether old hobbies can recruit again
articles.summary: Inside the 2026 push to refresh ham radio clubs, new spectrum rules, youth programs, and the argument over whether the hobby is growing or merely reorganizing.
